mnemonics.title: Exception Priority Order
mnemonics.content: RUSPDIF - Reset, Undefined, SWI, Prefetch Abort, Data Abort, IRQ, FIQ (Highest to lowest priority except FIQ being second-highest)

mnemonics.title: FIQ Advantages
mnemonics.content: FIQ: Faster (priority), Isolated (separate mode), Quicker (8 dedicated registers vs 1 in IRQ)

mnemonics.title: Interrupt Handling Sequence
mnemonics.content: DASSJSR: Detect → Acknowledge → Save State → Jump to ISR → Service → Restore → Return

mnemonics.title: Vector Table Offsets
mnemonics.content: 0x00-0x1C: Reset (0x00), Undef (0x04), SWI (0x08), PreAbort (0x0C), DataAbort (0x10), IRQ (0x18), FIQ (0x1C) → Each +4 except IRQ skips 0x14

acronyms.usage: Standard interrupt with shared registers and lower priority
High-priority interrupt with dedicated registers and separate mode
Stores return address with offset (PC+4/PC+8) based on exception type

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 7-story parking garage where each level (offset) holds a specific emergency vehicle (exception handler)
Picture 7 colored register sets (one per mode) - FIQ registers glow red for urgency
Visualize stack frames as nested Russian dolls - each ISR adds/removes a doll
A fire department hierarchy: Reset=Chief, FIQ=Fire Truck, IRQ=Ambulance, others=special units

analogies.analogy: Like ambulance response time - depends on traffic (current instruction), roadblocks (masked interrupts), and hospital prep (context save)
Regular 911 call (IRQ) vs Emergency SOS button (FIQ) - latter gets dedicated response team and priority
A building's emergency exit map - each arrow (vector) points to specific emergency protocol (ISR)
Like pausing a YouTube video to answer a call - browser saves playback state (registers), handles call (ISR), then resumes exactly where left off
